notes: Appears above the consonant. Example: KI (ཀི་) = 'kee'. The only vowel that goes below is U.
The only vowel mark that appears below the consonant. Example: KU (ཀུ་) = 'koo'.
Appears above the consonant. Example: KE (ཀེ་) = 'key'.
Appears above the consonant. Example: KO (ཀོ་) = 'ko'.

meaning: Row 1 Guttural, Col 1 Unaspirated, Low Tone
notes: Pronounced 'ka'. No puff of air. In Lhasa dialect, KA (Col 1) and GA (Col 3) are pronounced identically — both are plain 'ka.'

meaning: Row 1 Guttural, Col 2 Aspirated, High Tone
notes: Pronounced 'ka'. With a puff of air — like the 'k' in 'kick.' Aspiration and tone are the only distinctions between KA (Col 1, Low Tone) and KHA (Col 2, High Tone).

meaning: Row 1 Guttural, Col 3 Voiced, Low Tone
notes: Pronounced 'ka' (NOT 'ga'). In Lhasa dialect, all Col 3 Voiced letters merge with their Col 1 Unaspirated counterparts: GA=ka, JA=cha, DA=ta, BA=pa, DZA=tsa. The course marks 'ga' with a red X.

meaning: Row 1 Guttural, Col 4 Nasal, Low Tone
notes: Pronounced 'nga'. The guttural nasal — like the '-ng' at the end of 'ring.' Don't use the front of your tongue; the sound comes from the back of the throat.

meaning: Row 2 Palatal, Col 1 Unaspirated, Low Tone
notes: Pronounced 'cha'. Unaspirated palatal affricate. In Lhasa dialect, CA (Col 1) and JA (Col 3) are pronounced identically.

meaning: Row 2 Palatal, Col 2 Aspirated, High Tone
notes: Pronounced 'cha'. Aspirated — with a puff of air.

meaning: Row 2 Palatal, Col 3 Voiced, Low Tone
notes: Pronounced 'cha' (NOT 'ja'). In Lhasa dialect, Voiced (Col 3) letters merge with their Unaspirated (Col 1) counterparts. The course marks 'ja' with a red X.

meaning: Row 2 Palatal, Col 4 Nasal, Low Tone
notes: Pronounced 'nya'. Palatal nasal.

meaning: Row 3 Dental, Col 1 Unaspirated, Low Tone
notes: Pronounced 'ta'. Unaspirated dental stop. In Lhasa dialect, TA (Col 1) and DA (Col 3) are pronounced identically.

meaning: Row 3 Dental, Col 2 Aspirated, High Tone
notes: Pronounced 'ta'. Aspirated — with a puff of air.

meaning: Row 3 Dental, Col 3 Voiced, Low Tone
notes: Pronounced 'ta' (NOT 'da'). In Lhasa dialect, Voiced (Col 3) letters merge with their Unaspirated (Col 1) counterparts. The course marks 'da' with a red X.

meaning: Row 3 Dental, Col 4 Nasal, Low Tone
notes: Pronounced 'na'. Dental nasal.

meaning: Row 4 Labial, Col 1 Unaspirated, Low Tone
notes: Pronounced 'pa'. Unaspirated labial stop. In Lhasa dialect, PA (Col 1) and BA (Col 3) are pronounced identically.

meaning: Row 4 Labial, Col 2 Aspirated, High Tone
notes: Pronounced 'pa'. Aspirated — with a puff of air.

meaning: Row 4 Labial, Col 3 Voiced, Low Tone
notes: Pronounced 'pa' (NOT 'ba'). In Lhasa dialect, Voiced (Col 3) letters merge with their Unaspirated (Col 1) counterparts. The course marks 'ba' with a red X.

meaning: Row 4 Labial, Col 4 Nasal, Low Tone
notes: Pronounced 'ma'. Labial nasal.
Pronounced 'tsa'. Note: this course uses TZA for the unaspirated (Col 1) and TSA for the aspirated (Col 2) — the reverse of standard ACIP convention.
Pronounced 'tsa'. Aspirated — with a puff of air. Note: this course uses TSA for the aspirated (Col 2) and TZA for the unaspirated (Col 1) — the reverse of standard ACIP convention.
Pronounced 'tsa' (NOT 'dza'). Voiced (Col 3) merges with Unaspirated (Col 1) in Lhasa dialect — same pattern as GA=ka, JA=cha, DA=ta, BA=pa.
Pronounced 'wa'. Placed at Row 5 Col 4 in the grid but does not follow the nasal pattern of the other Col 4 letters. Functions as a semivowel.
Pronounced 'sha', Low Tone. Easy to confuse with SHA (ཤ་, Row 7), which is also 'sha' but Mid Tone. All Row 6 letters (ZHA, ZA, 'A, YA) are Low Tone.
Pronounced 'sa', Low Tone. Easy to confuse with SA (ས་, Row 7), which is also 'sa' but Mid Tone.
Pronounced 'ah', Low Tone. Easy to confuse with AA (ཨ་, Row 8), which is also 'ah' but Mid Tone. Also functions as a prefix and superscript in many words.
Pronounced 'ya', Low Tone.
Pronounced 'ra', Mid Tone. A rolled 'r' like Spanish — not the hard English 'r.' All Row 7 letters (RA, LA, SHA, SA) are Mid Tone.
Pronounced 'la', Mid Tone.
Pronounced 'sha', Mid Tone. Easy to confuse with ZHA (ཞ་, Row 6), which is also 'sha' but Low Tone.
Pronounced 'sa', Mid Tone. Easy to confuse with ZA (ཟ་, Row 6), which is also 'sa' but Low Tone.
Pronounced 'ha'. Row 8 has only two letters: HA and AA.
Pronounced 'ah'. The vowel carrier. Easy to confuse with 'A (འ་, Row 6), which is also 'ah' but Low Tone.

prompt: Head letter on a First Column Letter
notes: Spelling changes the meaning of the word, but the sound stays the same.

prompt: Head letter on a Second Column Letter
notes: Head letters will not appear on a second column letter.

prompt: Head letter on a Third Column Letter
notes: GA='ga', JA='ja', DA='da', BA='ba', DZA='dza'.

prompt: Head letter on a Fourth Column Letter
notes: In Tibetan, tone changes the word itself — not just emphasis as in English.
Strange exception — 'There is no Lhasa, only Hlasa.' Head letter HA on LA flips to make an 'hla' sound.
Strange exception — head letter DA on LA makes 'nda', not 'la'.

prompt: Subjoined RA on a first column letter
notes: The RA curls the tongue — tongue bends backwards to make the retroflex sound.

prompt: Subjoined RA on a second column letter
notes: Column II letters stay aspirated — with a puff of air.

prompt: Subjoined RA on a third column letter
notes: Column III letters stay voiced.
Frustrating exception — subjoined RA on MA does not follow the retroflex rule.
Frustrating exception — subjoined RA on SA does not follow the retroflex rule.
All combinations with subjoined LA make the same sound: high tone 'la'.
Strange exception — subjoined LA on ZA makes 'nda', not 'la'.
When WA is subjoined it makes an odd little shape but does not change the pronunciation of the letter at all.

meaning: Makes a longer vowel sound. Relates to Sanskrit
notes: Also called a'chung — 'little a'. Gets small and goes at the bottom. Only appears in Sanskrit words to indicate the long vowel sound.

prompt: Subjoined YA on a first row letter
notes: All three guttural letters produce 'kya': ཀྱ་ (KYA), ཁྱ་ (KHYA), གྱ་ (GYA) — all pronounced 'kya'.

prompt: Subjoined YA on a fourth row letter
meaning: Jumps up and sounds like a second row letter
notes: Labial row shifts to palatal sounds: ཕྱ་ (PHYA) = 'cha', བྱ་ (BYA) = 'cha', མྱ་ (MYA) = 'nya'.
e.g., ལར་ = 'lar'.
e.g., ལམ་ = 'lam', ལོམ་ = 'lom'.
Sounds like 'p', not 'b'. e.g., ལབ་ = 'lap', ལེབ་ = 'lep'.
e.g., ལིང་ = 'ling', རིང་ = 'ring'.
A final 'k' in Tibetan is more like a stop in the back of the throat — like the 'k' in 'back', 'look', 'truck'. e.g., ལག་ = 'lak', རིག་ = 'rik', ལུག་ = 'luk'.

Two effects in Central Dialect: 1) Vowel umlaut — inherent 'ah'→'ä' (sounds like 'ey'); shabkyu 'u'→'ü'; naro 'o'→'ö'; kiku (i) and drengbu (e) don't change. 2) DA shortens the vowel with a glottal stop. e.g., མེ་ 'me' → མེད་ 'me[stop]'.

Two effects in Central Dialect: 1) Vowel umlaut — inherent 'ah'→'ä' (sounds like 'ey'); shabkyu 'u'→'ü'; naro 'o'→'ö'; kiku (i) and drengbu (e) don't change. 2) SA lengthens the vowel. e.g., ལ་ 'la' → ལས་ 'lay'; ཆོ་ 'cho' → ཆོས་ 'chö' (lengthened).
Two effects: 1) Vowel umlaut — inherent 'ah'→'ä'; shabkyu 'u'→'ü'; naro 'o'→'ö'. 2) NA is fully pronounced. e.g., ཏོ་ 'to' → ཏོན་ 'tun'.
Two effects: 1) Vowel umlaut — inherent 'ah'→'ä'; shabkyu 'u'→'ü'; naro 'o'→'ö'. 2) LA is fully pronounced. e.g., རོ་ 'ro' → རོལ་ 'rul'.

The འ་ suffix is not pronounced and mostly does nothing on its own. It only relates to words with prefixes — revisit when studying prefixes. e.g., དགའར་ = 'ga' (ད་ is silent prefix, འ་ is the suffix).

prompt: Prefix letter before a First Column Letter
notes: Spelling changes the meaning of the word, but the sound stays the same. e.g., འཀར་, གཀར་, དཀར་ — all = 'kar'.

prompt: Prefix letter before a Second Column Letter
notes: Unlike head letters, prefixes CAN appear before Col II letters — head letters cannot. e.g., ཐུན་ and བཐུན་ — both = 'tun'.

prompt: Prefix letter before a Third Column Letter
notes: GA='ga', JA='ja', DA='da', BA='ba', DZA='dza'. e.g., གར་ = 'kar' (no prefix), མགར་ or དགར་ = 'gar' (with prefix).

prompt: Prefix letter before a Fourth Column Letter
notes: The letter sounds the same but rises to high tone. e.g., ངུ་ = 'ngu' (low tone), མངུ་ = 'ngu' (high tone).

MA and འ་ as prefixes cause a brief nasal sound before the syllable. The nasal matches the row of the following consonant: Row 1→'ng', Row 2→'ny', Row 3→'n', Row 4→'m', Row 5→'n'. Only heard after an open vowel (no suffix on the preceding syllable). e.g., མེ་ (fire) + མདར་ (arrow) = 'menda' (gun/rifle).
context: Prefix

notes: འ་ and མ་ as prefixes cause a brief nasal sound before the syllable. The nasal matches the row of the following consonant: Row 1→'ng-ga', Row 2→'ny-jel', Row 3→'n-da', Row 4→'m-ba', Row 5→'n-dza'. Only heard after an open vowel (no suffix on the preceding syllable).
context: Prefix

notes: Exception: DA (ད་) prefix + BA (བ་) creates a 'w' sound — all DA+BA combinations are irregular. DA + BA + NGA = 'wang' (power/empowerment).
Exception: DA (ད་) prefix + BA (བ་) = 'w' sound, plus subjoined YA + RA suffix. DA + BA + YA + RA = 'yar' (up/summer).
Exception: DA (ད་) prefix + BA (བ་) = 'w' sound, plus shabkyu vowel. DA + BA + U vowel = 'u' (head).
Exception: DA (ད་) prefix + BA (བ་) = 'w' sound, plus subjoined RA + LA suffix. DA + BA + RA + LA = 'shrel' (joint/connection).
Cardinal: GCIG. Ordinal: GCIG PA = first (though DANG PO is more common for "firstly"). GCIG PU = alone, only one. GCIG TU = into one, unified. GCIG CHAR = all at once.
Cardinal: GNYIS. Ordinal: GNYIS PA = second. GNYIS KA = both.
Cardinal: GSUM. Ordinal: GSUM PA = third. BSLAB PA GSUM = three trainings; SDE SNOD GSUM = three baskets (Tripiṭaka).
Cardinal: BZHI. Ordinal: BZHI PA = fourth. 'BYUNG BA BZHI = four elements; BDEN PA BZHI = four noble truths.
Cardinal: LNGA. Ordinal: LNGA PA = fifth. PHUNG PO LNGA = five aggregates; PHAR PHYIN LNGA = five perfections.
Cardinal: DRUG. Ordinal: DRUG PA = sixth. RNAM SHES DRUG = six consciousnesses; PHAR PHYIN DRUG = six perfections.
Cardinal: BDUN. Ordinal: BDUN PA = seventh. RIGS PA BDUN = sevenfold reasoning.
Cardinal: BRGYAD. Ordinal: BRGYAD PA = eighth. 'JIG RTEN CHOS BRGYAD = eight worldly concerns.
Cardinal: DGU. Ordinal: DGU PA = ninth.
Cardinal: BCU. Ordinal: BCU PA = tenth. BCU GCIG = eleven; BCU GNYIS = twelve.

meaning: type, kind, class, family, lineage; suitable, proper
notes: RIGS PA = logic, reasoning
TSOGS GNYIS = the two accumulations
Temporal adverb; PHYI = outer, after

meaning: thing, entity, real, actual; direct, primary
notes: DNGOS PO = thing/entity; DNGOS SU = directly
SKYON MED = faultless; opposite of YON TAN
Sanskrit: rūpa. First of the five aggregates
REG BYA = tangible object

meaning: element; to arise, emerge, come forth; source
notes: 'BYUNG BA BZHI = the four elements
context: Elemental sources of material existence. Even these apparently solid building blocks are MING TZAM when subjected to Madhyamaka analysis.

notes: Function: THOGS PA (obstruction)
Function: BSDUS PA (cohesion)
Function: SMIN PA (ripening)
Function: G.YO BA (movement)
Function of Earth; THOGS MED = unobstructed
context: Basis of the THOGS BCAS/THOGS MED distinction between material and immaterial phenomena.

notes: Function of Water; also = summary, compendium
Function of Fire; also: ripening of karma

meaning: to move, shake, sway; movement, motion
notes: Function of Wind; MI G.YO BA = unmoving
Used in dependent origination examples
adjective
RI RAB LHUN PO = Mount Meru
MANG = many + STONG = thousand
A qualified teacher guiding on the Buddhist path
Poetic epithet: "supreme bull/lord of the herd"

meaning: to speak, to say; speech; one who speaks
notes: verb/noun

meaning: to prostrate, to pay homage, to bow down
notes: PHYAG 'TSAL LO = "I prostrate!"
GSER = gold + RI = mountain
verb

meaning: means of knowing; that which makes known; indicator
notes: Contrast SHES BYA = object of knowledge
Key debate term; MTSAN GZHI = instance/example
context: Central to the question of RANG GI MTSAN NYID KYIS — whether phenomena are established by their own defining characteristic. Key fault line between Madhyamaka sub-schools.

meaning: action, activity, function; that which is to be done
notes: Also = verb (grammatical term)

meaning: to apply, connect, join; in applying
notes: Related to SBYOR BA = application, yoga
YON TAN LDAN PA = endowed with qualities
One of two types of visible form (with KHA DOG = color). Pronounced approx. "yib."

meaning: birth, arising, production; to be born
notes: 11th link of dependent origination; SKYE MED = unborn
context: Paired with 'GAG PA as SKYE 'GAG — the mark of all conditioned phenomena. Its negation SKYE BA MED PA points to the unconditioned and to emptiness.

meaning: mutual contact, meeting each other reciprocally
notes: PHAN TSUN = mutual; PHRAD pronounced approx. "tré"
Sanskrit: avidyā. 1st link of dependent origination; root of suffering
context: The fundamental misapprehension that assents to and grasps at the appearance of inherent existence (KUN BRTAGS) as if it were real in dependent phenomena (GZHAN DBANG). Phenomena appear to exist inherently — MA RIG PA does not create this appearance but mistakes it for reality, grasping at it as truly established. The DGAG BYA was never there — MA RIG PA takes the appearance for the real.

notes: Sanskrit: vijñāna. 3rd link; RNAM SHES TSOGS DRUG = six consciousnesses

meaning: name and name-bearer; word and referent
notes: MING = name + MING CAN = that which has a name
context: Name and name-bearer are mutually dependent (RTEN 'BREL) — neither established independently. The referent searched for behind the label is MI RNYED PA.

notes: Things appear inherent but are actually empty
context: The gap between SNANG TSUL (how things appear) and GNAS TSUL (how things are) — root of GNYIS SNANG.

meaning: even a little, even slightly; not at all
notes: Used in negations: CUNG ZAD KYANG MED = not even a bit
context: Relates to GANG LA NYES PA MA ("not a drop") — the proclamation of primordial purity.

notes: Elements don't exist independently from each other

meaning: when gathered/combined; at the time of coming together
notes: 'DUS = gathered
Contrast NANG DON (inner meaning)
Limiting particle
Suffix: "one who has"; similar to LDAN PA
Concessive particle
'JIGS = fear + MED = without
MED = not exist + PAR = in manner of
DE + S (instrumental) + NA (conditional). Sanskrit: tasmāt.
context: Logical connective at the hinge between premise and conclusion. Stronger and more assertive than DE LTAR YIN NA.

meaning: first; at the outset; first of all
notes: THOG MA = the first + MAR (adverbial particle)
context: Classic sequence: THOG MAR... DE NAS... MTHAR = first... then... finally.

meaning: if that is so; if it is like that; that being the case
notes: DE LTAR = like that/thus + YIN + NA = if/when
context: More conditional/dialogical than DES NA — often at the hinge after an objection or concession.

meaning: in accordance with; just as; like; in the manner of
notes: BZHIN = accordance + DU (adverbial). Related to JI BZHIN.
context: Acting BZHIN DU — in accordance with GNAS TSUL (how things are) rather than SNANG TSUL (how they appear) — is a concise description of what practice is for.

meaning: to what; in what; with regard to what; whatever
notes: GANG = what/which + LA = locative/dative. Emphatic: GA LA.
context: The GANG family (GANG ZHE NA, GANG ZHIG, GANG LA, GANG DU, GANG LTAR) does structural work throughout philosophical Tibetan — anchoring definitions, framing rhetorical questions.

notes: Deity name/epithet (possibly a protector deity)

meaning: the mind/attitude of renunciation; the thought of definite emergence
notes: NGES = definite; 'BYUNG = arise/emerge; BSAM PA = thought/intention
context: First of the three principal aspects of the path. Not just knowing about renunciation but a mind fully seized and permeated by it — requiring ZIN PAR.

meaning: having grasped; mind imbued with (an idea)
notes: Past stem of 'DZIN PA + PAR. Archaic usage.
context: Old term: a mind thoroughly taken up by an idea — not mere intellectual acquaintance but being permeated by it.

meaning: praise by others; being praised by others
notes: GZHAN = other; GYIS = by; BSTOD PA = praise
context: One of the eight worldly concerns. Paired with SMAD PA (blame). The practitioner works to regard both with equanimity.

acip: NGAN SONG DU SKYES KYI DOGS NAS
meaning: out of fear of being reborn in the lower realms
notes: NGAN SONG = lower realms; DOGS PA = fear/apprehension; NAS = from/on basis of
context: Motivating dread of the initial-scope practitioner (SKYES BU CHUNG NGU) in the Lam Rim.

meaning: ethics motivated by fear and the wish for protection
notes: 'JIGS = fear; SKYOBS = protection; TSUL KHRIMS = ethics (Sanskrit: śīla)
context: A preliminary grade of ethical motivation. Higher grades: ethics for liberation, or for the benefit of all beings.

notes: From THOR BA = to scatter, disperse
context: Genre designation for unsystematic collections — things gathered rather than composed as a unified treatise.

notes: GSUNG = honorific speech; THOR BU = scattered/miscellaneous
context: A recognized Tibetan literary genre: informal remarks of a lama compiled by disciples without systematic arrangement.

meaning: personal oral instruction; direct counsel from a teacher
notes: ZHAL = honorific mouth/face; GDAMS = instruction, advice
context: Emphasizes the personal, face-to-face quality of transmission. Carries the teacher's GDENG as well as information. Distinct from MAN NGAG and GSUNG THOR BU.

acip: GANG ZHIG LEGS GSUNG DAM CHOS 'DUL BA
meaning: one who excellently teaches the holy Dharma of Vinaya
notes: GANG ZHIG = whoever; LEGS = excellently; DAM CHOS = holy Dharma; 'DUL BA = Vinaya
context: 'DUL BA literally means "that which tames" — the monastic code as vehicle of self-discipline and foundation of the path.

notes: SHES = to know + BYA = gerundive nominalizer. Contrast: SHES BYED (means of knowing).
context: In Buddhist epistemology (TSAD MA): any phenomenon apprehensible by valid cognition. Everything that exists is a SHES BYA.

meaning: that which can be proven/established; a demonstrable basis
notes: GZHI = basis + GRUB = proven/established
context: The subject of debate must be GZHI GRUB — provable through valid cognition — before analysis begins. Negate too much = CHAD LTAR; too little = SGRO 'DOGS.

meaning: in explaining; in the context of teaching
notes: 'CHAD PA = to explain/expound + LA (locative/contextual)
context: Part of the triad: 'CHAD PA (teaching) / THOS PA (hearing) / BSGOM PA (meditation).

meaning: refutation and establishment; what to negate and what to posit
notes: DGAG PA = to negate; BZHAG PA = to posit/establish
context: The paired operations of Madhyamaka analysis. Precision of the DGAG BYA is everything — avoiding both CHAD LTAR and SGRO 'DOGS.

notes: Adds SPONG BA (to abandon/relinquish) to DGAG BZHAG
context: Moves from philosophical analysis into lived transformation — connects to LTA SGOM SPYOD (view, meditation, conduct).

meaning: the literal meaning; as it literally says
notes: SGRA = sound/word; JI BZHIN = just as/exactly as
context: If a statement is not SGRA JI BZHIN PA, the commentator asks GANG LA DGONGS. Contrasts with DRANG DON (provisional) and NGES DON (definitive).

notes: RGYU = cause + MTSAN = mark/characteristic. Formula: RGYU MTSAN GANG ZHE NA = "What is the reason?"
context: Broader than technical debate term GTAN TSIGS — covers any explanatory basis or justification.

meaning: other-powered; dependent on another; the dependent nature
notes: Sanskrit: paratantra. 2nd of the three Yogācāra natures.
context: The dependent flow of experience arising through causes and conditions. Equation: GZHAN DBANG − KUN BRTAGS = YONGS GRUB.

meaning: the perfectly established nature; perfect reality
notes: Sanskrit: pariniṣpanna. 3rd of the three Yogācāra natures.
context: Emptiness itself — GZHAN DBANG seen without KUN BRTAGS. Not a separate realm but dependent nature as it actually is. The stable ultimate truth that remains when conceptual fabrication is cleared away.

meaning: the imagined/imputed nature; thoroughly fabricated by conceptual thought
notes: Sanskrit: parikalpita. 1st of the three Yogācāra natures.
context: False superimposition of inherent existence onto GZHAN DBANG. Like a snake mistaken for a rope — purely fabricated, MI RNYED PA. Equation: GZHAN DBANG − KUN BRTAGS = YONGS GRUB.

meaning: arising and ceasing; production and cessation
notes: Contracted compound of SKYE BA + 'GAG PA
context: The defining characteristic of all conditioned phenomena. Its negation MI SKYE MI 'GAG is the opening move of Nāgārjuna's Mūlamadhyamakakārikā.

notes: Sanskrit: nirodha. Paired with SKYE BA as SKYE 'GAG.
context: All conditioned phenomena have SKYE BA and 'GAG PA. The unconditioned ('DUS MA BYAS) is MI SKYE MI 'GAG — neither arising nor ceasing.

notes: Literally "to throw a burden onto." SKUR = burden; 'DEBS = to throw.
context: One of the two errors of wrong view — paired with SGRO 'DOGS (superimposition). SKUR 'DEBS = denying conventional existence = CHAD LTAR. The DGAG must be precise enough to avoid both.

meaning: by its own defining characteristic; through its own intrinsic nature
notes: RANG = self/own; MTSAN NYID = characteristic; KYIS = instrumental.
context: The precise object of Madhyamaka negation. Svātantrikas accept this conventionally; Prāsaṅgikas reject it even conventionally. Searching RANG GI MTSAN NYID KYIS → MI RNYED PA → STONG PA NYID.

meaning: it has been shown; it is demonstrated; thus it is taught
notes: BSTAN = past stem of "to show/teach" + TO (archaic assertive final particle).
context: Archaic form of DO. Signals early canonical texts or passages deliberately invoking archaic register — often in scriptural citations.

notes: Sanskrit: śīla. TSUL = manner + KHRIMS = law/code.
context: Foundation of the three higher trainings. If one's experienced world is shaped by karmic action, TSUL KHRIMS is the mechanism by which that reality is constructed.

notes: Sanskrit: anutpāda. Negation of SKYE BA.
context: Phenomena have no true arising from their own side. SKYE BA MED PA'I CHOS KYI BZOD PA — patient acceptance of non-arising — marks a high bodhisattva attainment.

meaning: with what intention; having what in mind; in reference to what
notes: GANG = what + LA = locative + DGONGS = honorific "to intend"
context: The hermeneutical question when a statement cannot be taken SGRA JI BZHIN PA. Full analysis: DGONGS GZHI (basis), DGONGS PA (intention), DGOS PA (purpose), DNGOS LA GNOD PA (contradiction of literal meaning).

meaning: dualistic appearance; the appearance of two; duality
notes: GNYIS = two + SNANG = appearance. Sanskrit: dvayābhāsa.
context: The experiential split between SNANG TSUL and GNAS TSUL. The entire path is the progressive resolution of GNYIS SNANG — its dissolution marks direct realization of emptiness.

meaning: like a cut; nihilistically; in the manner of annihilation
notes: CHAD = cut/severed + LTAR = like/in manner of
context: The extreme of nihilism — paired with RTAG LTAR (eternalism). Misapplying emptiness as "nothing exists" is CHAD LTAR. The Middle Way avoids both extremes.

notes: Full form: KHYED RANG. Polite 2nd person pronoun. Ordinary: KHYOD.
context: Standard form of address in debate — the Challenger addresses the Defender as KHYED RANG throughout the exchange.

meaning: not finding; unfindable; unable to find
notes: MI = negative + RNYED PA = to find/obtain
context: The crucial outcome of Madhyamaka analysis: searching for inherent existence and finding nothing. This unfindability IS the emptiness — not a failure but the discovery. The conventional phenomenon remains; its inherent existence was never there.

meaning: support, basis, foundation; that which supports
notes: Paired with BRTEN (that which depends upon)
context: Together RTEN/BRTEN articulate RTEN 'BREL (dependent origination, Sanskrit: pratītyasamutpāda). Every phenomenon is BRTEN — it has no RTEN within itself — its inherent ground is MI RNYED PA.

meaning: that which relies upon; the supported; to depend on
notes: Paired with RTEN (support/basis)
context: See RTEN. The structure of RTEN/BRTEN — external dependence without internal ground — is another way of arriving at emptiness.

meaning: unconditioned; uncompounded; not made by causes and conditions
notes: Sanskrit: asaṃskṛta. Opposite of 'DUS BYAS. Classic examples: NAM MKHA' (space), nirvāṇa.
context: Even unconditioned phenomena are MING TZAM — NAM MKHA' YANG NI MING TZAM. No category of phenomenon escapes Prāsaṅgika analysis.

meaning: merely a name; only nominally; just a label
notes: MING = name + TZAM = merely/only
context: The Prāsaṅgika destination: phenomena exist only as nominal designations. Search behind the label → MI RNYED PA → STONG PA NYID. What remains is MING TZAM — avoiding CHAD LTAR while negating inherent existence.

meaning: object of negation; that which is to be refuted; the negandum
notes: DGAG = negation + BYA = gerundive nominalizer. Sanskrit: pratiṣedhya.
context: The pivotal challenge of the view. Too broad = CHAD LTAR; too narrow = SGRO 'DOGS. The DGAG BYA (inherent existence) was never there — not removed but recognized as always absent. Something that is not there.

notes: THOGS = obstruction + BCAS = having/with. Opposite: THOGS MED.
context: Material phenomena (THOGS BCAS) vs. immaterial (THOGS MED = mind, space, emptiness). Maps onto GZUGS / GZUGS MED.

acip: NAM MKHA' YANG NI MING TZAM
meaning: even space is merely a name; space too is nothing but a label
notes: NAM MKHA' = space/sky; YANG NI = even/also (emphatic)
context: Space — the strongest candidate for independent existence as an unconditioned phenomenon — is also MING TZAM. Closes every escape route for inherent existence.

meaning: where could there be such a view? how could such a view exist?
notes: LTA = view; GA LA = rhetorical "where/how?" (emphatic negative); YOD = exists
context: Even the view of emptiness cannot be grasped as inherently existing. Nāgārjuna: clinging to emptiness itself is the subtlest SGRO 'DOGS.

meaning: name-bearer; that which has a name; the named/referent
notes: MING = name + CAN = possessing/having
context: MING and MING CAN are mutually dependent (RTEN 'BREL) — neither established independently. The referent behind the label is MI RNYED PA; yet conventionally both function — MING TZAM without CHAD LTAR.

meaning: harmed/invalidated by reasoning; contradicted by rational analysis
notes: RIGS PA = reasoning/logic + GNOD = to harm/invalidate
context: One of four criteria for identifying provisional (DRANG DON) meaning. In Madhyamaka: inherent existence is RIGS PAS GNOD — collapses under analysis → MI RNYED PA → MING TZAM.

meaning: "Not a drop" (lit. "wherein is there fault? — there is none")
notes: NYES PA = fault/transgression. MA = negative. Poetic/verse usage.
context: Proclamation of primordial purity (KA DAG): from the ultimate perspective not even a drop of inherent fault exists. The DGAG BYA was never there. Requires GDENG BROD — not mere intellectual assertion.

meaning: confidence, certainty, assurance; trust in one's realization
notes: From GDENG BA = to be confident, to have assurance
context: The inner certainty arising from genuine realization — self-validating, unshakeable, antidote to THE TSOM (doubt). A teacher transmits GDENG as well as information.

meaning: to proclaim with confidence; to roar one's realization
notes: GDENG = confidence + BROD = to proclaim/roar forth
context: GDENG is the inner certainty; GDENG BROD is that certainty given full voice — the lion's roar (SENG GE'I NGA RO). GANG LA NYES PA MA proclaimed as GDENG BROD: realization thrown out without reservation.

notes: GZHI = basis + GRUB = proven/established. Emphasis on epistemic provability.
context: Synonymous with CHOS, SHES BYA, YOD PA, and YUL — all coextensive. GZHI GRUB foregrounds epistemic status: it can be established through valid cognition and brought forward as a legitimate subject of inquiry. The subject of debate must be GZHI GRUB before analysis begins.

meaning: thing, existing thing, a teaching, reality, the truth, duty
notes: Sanskrit: dharma. From root dhṛ = to hold, to sustain. CHOS = whatever holds its own nature (RANG GI NGO BO 'DZIN PA).
context: One of the most semantically rich words in Tibetan — simultaneously phenomenon (anything that exists), Dharma (the Buddha's teaching), and right conduct. Synonymous with SHES BYA, GZHI GRUB, YOD PA, YUL. CHOS foregrounds ontological status: it holds its own nature. The teachings (CHOS) are teachings about phenomena (CHOS) — the ambiguity is a feature, not a bug.

notes: SHES = to know + BYA = gerundive nominalizer. Sanskrit: jñeya.
context: Synonymous with CHOS, GZHI GRUB, YOD PA, YUL. SHES BYA foregrounds cognitive accessibility: it falls within the scope of a knowing mind. In Dharmakīrti's epistemology, defined by causal efficacy — if something can perform a function it can be known, and vice versa. There is no gap between existence and knowability.

notes: Sanskrit: deṣa (place/region) and viṣaya (object/domain). Covers both spatial and cognitive senses.
context: Synonymous with CHOS, SHES BYA, GZHI GRUB, YOD PA. YUL foregrounds the relational aspect: not just that something exists and can be known, but that it stands as the object of a particular cognitive act. YUL CAN = subject (literally "that which has an object"). The YUL/YUL CAN structure is itself an expression of GNYIS SNANG (dualistic appearance).

notes: YOD = to exist, there is + PA (nominalizer). Opposite: MED PA (non-existent, absence).
context: The most basic member of the synonymy cluster — the bare existential affirmation. If something is YOD PA it is automatically CHOS, SHES BYA, GZHI GRUB, and YUL. In Madhyamaka the crucial question is not whether something is YOD PA but *how* — inherently (RANG GI MTSAN NYID KYIS) or merely nominally (MING TZAM). YOD PA by itself is neutral; the qualifier is everything.

notes: GZHAN = other + LUGS = system/position/tradition + DGAG PA = to refute/negate. LUGS carries the sense of a whole philosophical system, not just a single claim.
context: One of the two classic movements of Buddhist philosophical writing — paired with RANG LUGS SGRUB PA (establishing one's own position). Central to the Prāsaṅgika method: Nāgārjuna and Candrakīrti operate almost exclusively through DGAG PA, driving opponents toward unwanted consequences (THAL 'GYUR) rather than asserting positive theses. Whether Prāsaṅgikas have a RANG LUGS at all is itself one of the great debates in the tradition.

notes: Case 1 (Nominative): the subject of a verb. Case 8 (Vocative): direct address. No particle is added — the noun stands alone.

acip: SU RU TU DU NA RA LA
notes: Case 2 (Accusative/Direct Object — ex: at, to): the object of an action. Case 4 (Dative/Beneficial — ex: for): the recipient or beneficiary. Case 7 (Locative — ex: at, on, in): location or direction. Choice of particle determined by the final letter of the preceding syllable.

tibetan: གྱིས་ གིས་ ཀྱིས་ འིས་ ས་ ཡིས་
notes: Case 3 (Agentive/Instrumental — ex: by, with): marks the agent of a transitive verb or the instrument by which an action is performed. Choice of particle determined by the final letter of the preceding syllable.

Case 6 (Genitive/Possessive/Connective — ex: 's, of): marks possession, connection, or modification between nouns. One of the most frequent particles in Tibetan. Choice of particle determined by the final letter of the preceding syllable.

Case 5 (Ablative/Originative — ex: from, than, then): marks the source, origin, or point of departure of an action. Also used for comparison (than) and temporal sequence (then, after). NAS tends toward source/sequence; LAS toward origin/comparison.
